毎年1月7日にこどもの開運・健康・出世を祈り、裃姿のこどもによって執り行われる。古くは弓射りとも称し、源頼朝の奉納に始まるという伝承がある。
社前に設けられた射場は、18本の青竹を矢来に組んでヨシズを張り、その上に八方白眼（はっぽうにらみ）という特有の的（それぞれ異なる8つの眉と目を縦2列に描いた和紙を、大きな蛇の目の中央に張ったもの）である。弓は椿の木に麻の弦を張ったもの、矢は長さ1m程の篠竹に西之内の矢羽を付けたものを用いる。 射士は氏子の12歳以下の男児に限られ、射る順番は抽選によって決められる。1番手と2番手が、矢を交差させ、3本の大きな御幣が立っている的の上を越すように射り（山越し）、山越しがすむと、3番手と4番手が『一の目玉』を、5番手と6番手が『二の目玉』という具合に射り進む。
流鏑馬といっても馬には乗らず、多摩川流域に多くみられた「歩射」（御備射）の伝統を守っていたが、平成10年（[[1998年]]）からは八方白眼の幕の前に置いた木馬にまたがり、正方形の杉板の的に鏑矢を射るという、新しい様式が取り入れられている。 === 過去に行われていた祭事 === ; 曳船祭 六郷神社の曳船祭は、江戸時代から六郷川（多摩川河口域）で勇壮華麗にくりひろげられ、関東三大船祭りの一つといわれた。かつて羽田と対岸の大師河原が六郷神社の氏子であったため、宮神輿を御座船に乗せ、四艘の足船が長さ150尺（約45m）の大綱で曳き、その後から各町会の迎え船と送り船がのぼり旗を風になびかせ、囃子の音と無数の観覧船とともに川を下っていった。 ==文化財== ;狛犬 : 大田区指定有形文化財（金石文）。昭和55年3月19日指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} :神楽殿の前庭に置かれている狛犬は、[[貞享]]2年（[[1685年]]）に六郷中町の有志が願主となり、二世安楽を祈って奉納したもので、大田区内最古の狛犬である。石工は三右衛門。元は社殿前にあった。左側の呵形は耳を付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約50cm。右側の吽形は角を3つ付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約52cm。 ;六郷神社獅子舞 :大田区指定無形民俗文化財。平成19年指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} ; 六郷神社の流鏑馬 :東京都指定無形民俗文化財（民俗芸能）。昭和38年指定。{{sfn|六郷神社の流鏑馬}} === その他 === * 社宝四神 - 六郷八幡宮の四神については、[[天保]]3年（[[1831年]]）の『東都歳事記』（[[斎藤月岑]]著）にも記述がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ; 注釈 ; 出典 {{reflist|1}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ota/200148/|title=六郷神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref=harv}} * {{Cite web|和書|title=大田区指定文化財一覧|website=大田区|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|大田区指定文化財一覧}} |url=https://www.city.ota.tokyo.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai_hogo/shitei_torokubunkazai/bunkazai_list.files/20190401bunka_ku_shitei.pdf}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=478|title=六郷神社の流鏑馬|website=東京都文化財情報データベース|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|六郷神社の流鏑馬}}}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html 六郷神社公式サイト] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ろくこうしんしや}} [[Category:大田区の神社]] [[Category:武蔵国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

{{神社 |名称 = 六郷神社| |画像 = [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
六郷神社の鳥居と神門 {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} |所在地 = [[東京都]][[大田区]][[東六郷]]3-10-18{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |ISO = JP-13 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 32|緯度秒 = 39.00 |経度度 =139|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 47.00 |祭神 = [[誉田別尊]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |社格 = 旧郷社 |創建 = 伝[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[6月3日]] {{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |神事 = [[流鏑馬]]祭（[[1月7日]]） |地図 = Japan Tokyo }} '''六郷神社'''（ろくごうじんじゃ）は、[[東京都]][[大田区]][[東六郷]]にある[[神社]]である。六郷一円の総鎮守として伝えられる。創建は古く、平安時代後期とされる。 [[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[郷社]]。[[延喜式神名帳]]では「薭田神社1字目は「草冠に稗」。 [[武蔵国]][[荏原郡]]鎮座」となっていた。 祭神は[[八幡大神]]（[[誉田別尊]]）。[[八幡宮]]であるが、現在では八幡三神のうち[[神功皇后]]と[[比売大神]]の二柱は祀られておらず、誉田別尊のみを祭神とする。 ==歴史== === 創建 === 社伝によると[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）、[[源頼義]]、[[源義家|源（八幡太郎）義家]]父子が、この地の大杉の梢高くに[[源氏]]の白旗をかかげて軍勢をつのり、[[石清水八幡宮]]に武運長久を祈願したところ軍の士気が大いに高まり、 [[前九年の役]]で勝利したため、凱旋後に石清水八幡宮の分霊を勧請し八幡宮を創建したとされる。江戸時代には「六郷八幡宮」と称していた。 === 概史 === [[文治]]5年（[[1189年]]）、[[源頼朝]]もまた、[[源義経]]および[[奥州藤原氏]]の征討（[[奥州合戦]]）に際し祖先の吉例にならって白旗を立て、戦での勝利を祈願した。[[建久]]2年（[[1191年]]）に[[梶原景時]]に命じて[[社殿]]を造営。 現在、社宝となっている雌獅子頭（めじしがしら）と境内に残る浄水石はこのときに頼朝が奉献したもの、また神門前の太鼓橋は景時が寄進したものといわれている。 [[天正]]19年（[[1591年]]）、[[徳川家康]]は、[[神領]]として十八石を寄進する[[朱印状]]を発給した。 [[慶長]]5年（[[1600年]]）、家康は神社近傍を流れる[[多摩川|六郷川]]に架橋（[[六郷橋|六郷大橋]]）を命じ[http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/kawakoku/pdf/oyabashira.pdf 旧六郷橋 親柱物語]、その竣工を祈って願文を奉り、またこの神社の[[神輿]]によって渡初式を行ったと伝えられている。このように徳川家との縁が深いため、神紋として八幡宮の[[巴紋]]と[[三つ葉葵]]紋を用いている。 時期については定かではないが、六郷神社では元来、他の八幡社と同様に八幡三神（誉田別尊、神功皇后、比売大神）を祀っていた。 しかし、あるときの曳船祭で、一座の神輿が[[東京湾]]の対岸である[[上総国]]に流されてしまい、もう一座の神輿はたいへんな荒神で、しばしば祟りを受けたので土中に埋めてしまったと江戸時代の書に書かれている（[[享保]]4年（[[1719年]]）造営の本殿は三神を祀る建築様式になっていることから、上記の故事が起きたのはそれ以後ではないかとされている）。 江戸時代には、[[東海道]]を隔てた西側の[[宝珠院 (大田区)|宝珠院]]が[[別当寺]]であったが、[[明治維新]]により廃された。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）、東京府郷社となる。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に六郷神社と改称した。 [[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）、鎮座九百三十年祭執行。 ==境内== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|社殿]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|神橋]] * 社殿 : 昭和62年（1987年）の鎮座九百三十年を期に建て替えられたが、享保4年（1719年）の造営の本殿は改修された。 : 古くから本殿正面に掲げられた扁額の「八幡宮」の文字は、源忠持筆と記されている（年代不詳）。 * 神門 : 神橋を前にした切妻造。左右に透塀を連ねて玉垣をめぐらしている。 * 神橋 : 梶原景時の寄進と伝えられる石造の太鼓橋が残っている。 * 社務所 : [[平成]]7年（[[1995年]]）の造営。 *白旗の杉 : 拝殿のすぐ東側にあった白旗の杉は、樹齢1000年といわれ、御神木として崇められていたが、[[大正]]11年（[[1922年]]）に借しくも枯れ、現在は根株のみが残る。 *手水舎 : 切妻造。表参道の東側にある。 {{clear}} ==境内社== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|境内社]] * 稲荷神社 * 氷川神社 * 天祖神社 * 三柱神社 {{clear}} == 祭事 == [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|神楽殿]] === 例大祭 === ; 例祭 毎年6月3日に行われ、6月の第1金曜日の神社神輿遷御祭では、六郷14町会約40基の神輿が次々と神社に担ぎ込まれ、みたま入れが行われる。 翌土曜日には、数百年の伝統を持つ獅子舞の奉納が神楽殿で行われ、神獅子巡行、六郷ばやしの奉納がある。翌日曜日、御神幸祭で神輿に乗せたご神体が六郷14町会を巡行しクライマックスを迎える。 ; 宮神輿 古来より六郷神社の神輿ぶりは勇壮をもって知られており、一之神輿は、昭和7年（[[1932年]]）に新調したもので、台幅1.21m、高さ1.96m、重量450kg（担ぎ棒を含まず）。二之神輿は、[[文久]]3年（[[1863年]]）の古い神輿を、昭和55年（[[1980年]]）に氏子青年会が修復したものである。 === 流鏑馬 === ; 七草こども流鏑馬祭 毎年1月7日にこどもの開運・健康・出世を祈り、裃姿のこどもによって執り行われる。古くは弓射りとも称し、源頼朝の奉納に始まるという伝承がある。
社前に設けられた射場は、18本の青竹を矢来に組んでヨシズを張り、その上に八方白眼（はっぽうにらみ）という特有の的（それぞれ異なる8つの眉と目を縦2列に描いた和紙を、大きな蛇の目の中央に張ったもの）である。弓は椿の木に麻の弦を張ったもの、矢は長さ1m程の篠竹に西之内の矢羽を付けたものを用いる。 射士は氏子の12歳以下の男児に限られ、射る順番は抽選によって決められる。1番手と2番手が、矢を交差させ、3本の大きな御幣が立っている的の上を越すように射り（山越し）、山越しがすむと、3番手と4番手が『一の目玉』を、5番手と6番手が『二の目玉』という具合に射り進む。
流鏑馬といっても馬には乗らず、多摩川流域に多くみられた「歩射」（御備射）の伝統を守っていたが、平成10年（[[1998年]]）からは八方白眼の幕の前に置いた木馬にまたがり、正方形の杉板の的に鏑矢を射るという、新しい様式が取り入れられている。 === 過去に行われていた祭事 === ; 曳船祭 六郷神社の曳船祭は、江戸時代から六郷川（多摩川河口域）で勇壮華麗にくりひろげられ、関東三大船祭りの一つといわれた。かつて羽田と対岸の大師河原が六郷神社の氏子であったため、宮神輿を御座船に乗せ、四艘の足船が長さ150尺（約45m）の大綱で曳き、その後から各町会の迎え船と送り船がのぼり旗を風になびかせ、囃子の音と無数の観覧船とともに川を下っていった。 ==文化財== ;狛犬 : 大田区指定有形文化財（金石文）。昭和55年3月19日指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} :神楽殿の前庭に置かれている狛犬は、[[貞享]]2年（[[1685年]]）に六郷中町の有志が願主となり、二世安楽を祈って奉納したもので、大田区内最古の狛犬である。石工は三右衛門。元は社殿前にあった。左側の呵形は耳を付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約50cm。右側の吽形は角を3つ付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約52cm。 ;六郷神社獅子舞 :大田区指定無形民俗文化財。平成19年指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} ; 六郷神社の流鏑馬 :東京都指定無形民俗文化財（民俗芸能）。昭和38年指定。{{sfn|六郷神社の流鏑馬}} === その他 === * 社宝四神 - 六郷八幡宮の四神については、[[天保]]3年（[[1831年]]）の『東都歳事記』（[[斎藤月岑]]著）にも記述がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ; 注釈 ; 出典 {{reflist|1}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ota/200148/|title=六郷神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref=harv}} * {{Cite web|和書|title=大田区指定文化財一覧|website=大田区|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|大田区指定文化財一覧}} |url=https://www.city.ota.tokyo.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai_hogo/shitei_torokubunkazai/bunkazai_list.files/20190401bunka_ku_shitei.pdf}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=478|title=六郷神社の流鏑馬|website=東京都文化財情報データベース|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|六郷神社の流鏑馬}}}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html 六郷神社公式サイト] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ろくこうしんしや}} [[Category:大田区の神社]] [[Category:武蔵国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

しかし、あるときの曳船祭で、一座の神輿が[[東京湾]]の対岸である[[上総国]]に流されてしまい、もう一座の神輿はたいへんな荒神で、しばしば祟りを受けたので土中に埋めてしまったと江戸時代の書に書かれている（[[享保]]4年（[[1719年]]）造営の本殿は三神を祀る建築様式になっていることから、上記の故事が起きたのはそれ以後ではないかとされている）。 江戸時代には、[[東海道]]を隔てた西側の[[宝珠院 (大田区)|宝珠院]]が[[別当寺]]であったが、[[明治維新]]により廃された。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）、東京府郷社となる。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に六郷神社と改称した。 [[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）、鎮座九百三十年祭執行。 ==境内== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|社殿]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|神橋]] * 社殿 : 昭和62年（1987年）の鎮座九百三十年を期に建て替えられたが、享保4年（1719年）の造営の本殿は改修された。 : 古くから本殿正面に掲げられた扁額の「八幡宮」の文字は、源忠持筆と記されている（年代不詳）。 * 神門 : 神橋を前にした切妻造。左右に透塀を連ねて玉垣をめぐらしている。 * 神橋 : 梶原景時の寄進と伝えられる石造の太鼓橋が残っている。 * 社務所 : [[平成]]7年（[[1995年]]）の造営。 *白旗の杉 : 拝殿のすぐ東側にあった白旗の杉は、樹齢1000年といわれ、御神木として崇められていたが、[[大正]]11年（[[1922年]]）に借しくも枯れ、現在は根株のみが残る。 *手水舎 : 切妻造。表参道の東側にある。 {{clear}} ==境内社== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|境内社]] * 稲荷神社 * 氷川神社 * 天祖神社 * 三柱神社 {{clear}} == 祭事 == [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|神楽殿]] === 例大祭 === ; 例祭 毎年6月3日に行われ、6月の第1金曜日の神社神輿遷御祭では、六郷14町会約40基の神輿が次々と神社に担ぎ込まれ、みたま入れが行われる。 翌土曜日には、数百年の伝統を持つ獅子舞の奉納が神楽殿で行われ、神獅子巡行、六郷ばやしの奉納がある。翌日曜日、御神幸祭で神輿に乗せたご神体が六郷14町会を巡行しクライマックスを迎える。 ; 宮神輿 古来より六郷神社の神輿ぶりは勇壮をもって知られており、一之神輿は、昭和7年（[[1932年]]）に新調したもので、台幅1.21m、高さ1.96m、重量450kg（担ぎ棒を含まず）。二之神輿は、[[文久]]3年（[[1863年]]）の古い神輿を、昭和55年（[[1980年]]）に氏子青年会が修復したものである。 === 流鏑馬 === ; 七草こども流鏑馬祭 毎年1月7日にこどもの開運・健康・出世を祈り、裃姿のこどもによって執り行われる。古くは弓射りとも称し、源頼朝の奉納に始まるという伝承がある。
社前に設けられた射場は、18本の青竹を矢来に組んでヨシズを張り、その上に八方白眼（はっぽうにらみ）という特有の的（それぞれ異なる8つの眉と目を縦2列に描いた和紙を、大きな蛇の目の中央に張ったもの）である。弓は椿の木に麻の弦を張ったもの、矢は長さ1m程の篠竹に西之内の矢羽を付けたものを用いる。 射士は氏子の12歳以下の男児に限られ、射る順番は抽選によって決められる。1番手と2番手が、矢を交差させ、3本の大きな御幣が立っている的の上を越すように射り（山越し）、山越しがすむと、3番手と4番手が『一の目玉』を、5番手と6番手が『二の目玉』という具合に射り進む。
流鏑馬といっても馬には乗らず、多摩川流域に多くみられた「歩射」（御備射）の伝統を守っていたが、平成10年（[[1998年]]）からは八方白眼の幕の前に置いた木馬にまたがり、正方形の杉板の的に鏑矢を射るという、新しい様式が取り入れられている。 === 過去に行われていた祭事 === ; 曳船祭 六郷神社の曳船祭は、江戸時代から六郷川（多摩川河口域）で勇壮華麗にくりひろげられ、関東三大船祭りの一つといわれた。かつて羽田と対岸の大師河原が六郷神社の氏子であったため、宮神輿を御座船に乗せ、四艘の足船が長さ150尺（約45m）の大綱で曳き、その後から各町会の迎え船と送り船がのぼり旗を風になびかせ、囃子の音と無数の観覧船とともに川を下っていった。 ==文化財== ;狛犬 : 大田区指定有形文化財（金石文）。昭和55年3月19日指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} :神楽殿の前庭に置かれている狛犬は、[[貞享]]2年（[[1685年]]）に六郷中町の有志が願主となり、二世安楽を祈って奉納したもので、大田区内最古の狛犬である。石工は三右衛門。元は社殿前にあった。左側の呵形は耳を付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約50cm。右側の吽形は角を3つ付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約52cm。 ;六郷神社獅子舞 :大田区指定無形民俗文化財。平成19年指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} ; 六郷神社の流鏑馬 :東京都指定無形民俗文化財（民俗芸能）。昭和38年指定。{{sfn|六郷神社の流鏑馬}} === その他 === * 社宝四神 - 六郷八幡宮の四神については、[[天保]]3年（[[1831年]]）の『東都歳事記』（[[斎藤月岑]]著）にも記述がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ; 注釈 ; 出典 {{reflist|1}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ota/200148/|title=六郷神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref=harv}} * {{Cite web|和書|title=大田区指定文化財一覧|website=大田区|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|大田区指定文化財一覧}} |url=https://www.city.ota.tokyo.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai_hogo/shitei_torokubunkazai/bunkazai_list.files/20190401bunka_ku_shitei.pdf}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=478|title=六郷神社の流鏑馬|website=東京都文化財情報データベース|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|六郷神社の流鏑馬}}}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html 六郷神社公式サイト] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ろくこうしんしや}} [[Category:大田区の神社]] [[Category:武蔵国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

{{神社 |名称 = 六郷神社| |画像 = [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
六郷神社の鳥居と神門
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} |所在地 = [[東京都]][[大田区]][[東六郷]]3-10-18{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |ISO = JP-13 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 32|緯度秒 = 39.00 |経度度 =139|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 47.00 |祭神 = [[誉田別尊]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |社格 = 旧[[郷社]] |創建 = 伝[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[6月3日]] {{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |神事 = [[流鏑馬]]祭（[[1月7日]]） |地図 = Japan Tokyo city }} '''六郷神社'''（ろくごうじんじゃ）は、[[東京都]][[大田区]][[東六郷]]にある[[神社]]である。六郷一円の総鎮守として伝えられる。創建は古く、平安時代後期とされる。 [[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[郷社]]。[[延喜式神名帳]]では「薭田神社1字目は「草冠に稗」。 [[武蔵国]][[荏原郡]]鎮座」となっていた。 祭神は[[八幡大神]]（[[誉田別尊]]）。[[八幡宮]]であるが、現在では八幡三神のうち[[神功皇后]]と[[比売大神]]の二柱は祀られておらず、誉田別尊のみを祭神とする。 ==歴史== === 創建 === 社伝によると[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）、[[源頼義]]、[[源義家|源（八幡太郎）義家]]父子が、この地の大杉の梢高くに[[源氏]]の白旗をかかげて軍勢をつのり、[[石清水八幡宮]]に武運長久を祈願したところ軍の士気が大いに高まり、 [[前九年の役]]で勝利したため、凱旋後に石清水八幡宮の分霊を勧請し八幡宮を創建したとされる。江戸時代には「六郷八幡宮」と称していた。 === 概史 === [[文治]]5年（[[1189年]]）、[[源頼朝]]もまた、[[源義経]]および[[奥州藤原氏]]の征討（[[奥州合戦]]）に際し祖先の吉例にならって白旗を立て、戦での勝利を祈願した。[[建久]]2年（[[1191年]]）に[[梶原景時]]に命じて[[社殿]]を造営。 現在、社宝となっている雌獅子頭（めじしがしら）と境内に残る浄水石はこのときに頼朝が奉献したもの、また神門前の太鼓橋は景時が寄進したものといわれている。 [[天正]]19年（[[1591年]]）、[[徳川家康]]は、[[神領]]として十八石を寄進する[[朱印状]]を発給した。 [[慶長]]5年（[[1600年]]）、家康は神社近傍を流れる[[多摩川|六郷川]]に架橋（[[六郷橋|六郷大橋]]）を命じ[http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/kawakoku/pdf/oyabashira.pdf 旧六郷橋 親柱物語]、その竣工を祈って願文を奉り、またこの神社の[[神輿]]によって渡初式を行ったと伝えられている。このように徳川家との縁が深いため、神紋として八幡宮の[[巴紋]]と[[葵紋#三つ葉葵|三つ葉葵]]紋を用いている。 時期については定かではないが、六郷神社では元来、他の八幡社と同様に八幡三神（誉田別尊、神功皇后、比売大神）を祀っていた。 しかし、あるときの曳船祭で、一座の神輿が[[東京湾]]の対岸である[[上総国]]に流されてしまい、もう一座の神輿はたいへんな荒神で、しばしば祟りを受けたので土中に埋めてしまったと江戸時代の書に書かれている（[[享保]]4年（[[1719年]]）造営の本殿は三神を祀る建築様式になっていることから、上記の故事が起きたのはそれ以後ではないかとされている）。 江戸時代には、[[東海道]]を隔てた西側の[[宝珠院 (大田区)|宝珠院]]が[[別当寺]]であったが、[[明治維新]]により廃された。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）、東京府郷社となる。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に六郷神社と改称した。 [[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）、鎮座九百三十年祭執行。 ==境内== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, 天祖神社 * 三柱神社 {{clear}} == 祭事 == [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|神楽殿]] === 例大祭 === ; 例祭 毎年6月3日に行われ、6月の第1金曜日の神社神輿遷御祭では、六郷14町会約40基の神輿が次々と神社に担ぎ込まれ、みたま入れが行われる。 |ISO = JP-13 |緯度度 = 35|緯度分 = 32|緯度秒 = 39.1 |経度度 =139|経度分 = 42|経度秒 = 47.2 |祭神 = [[誉田別尊]]{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |社格 = 旧[[郷社]] |創建 = 伝[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）{{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |本殿 = |別名 = |札所等 = |例祭 = [[6月3日]] {{sfn|東京都神社庁}} |神事 = [[流鏑馬]]祭（[[1月7日]]） |地図 = Japan Tokyo city }} '''六郷神社'''（ろくごうじんじゃ）は、[[東京都]][[大田区]][[東六郷]]にある[[神社]]である。六郷一円の総鎮守として伝えられる。創建は古く、平安時代後期とされる。 [[近代社格制度|旧社格]]は[[郷社]]。[[延喜式神名帳]]では「薭田神社1字目は「草冠に稗」。 [[武蔵国]][[荏原郡]]鎮座」となっていた。 祭神は[[八幡大神]]（[[誉田別尊]]）。[[八幡宮]]であるが、現在では八幡三神のうち[[神功皇后]]と[[比売大神]]の二柱は祀られておらず、誉田別尊のみを祭神とする。 ==歴史== === 創建 === 社伝によると[[天喜]]5年（[[1057年]]）、[[源頼義]]、[[源義家|源（八幡太郎）義家]]父子が、この地の大杉の梢高くに[[源氏]]の白旗をかかげて軍勢をつのり、[[石清水八幡宮]]に武運長久を祈願したところ軍の士気が大いに高まり、 [[前九年の役]]で勝利したため、凱旋後に石清水八幡宮の分霊を勧請し八幡宮を創建したとされる。江戸時代には「六郷八幡宮」と称していた。 === 概史 === [[文治]]5年（[[1189年]]）、[[源頼朝]]もまた、[[源義経]]および[[奥州藤原氏]]の征討（[[奥州合戦]]）に際し祖先の吉例にならって白旗を立て、戦での勝利を祈願した。[[建久]]2年（[[1191年]]）に[[梶原景時]]に命じて[[社殿]]を造営。 現在、社宝となっている雌獅子頭（めじしがしら）と境内に残る浄水石はこのときに頼朝が奉献したもの、また神門前の太鼓橋は景時が寄進したものといわれている。 [[天正]]19年（[[1591年]]）、[[徳川家康]]は、[[神領]]として十八石を寄進する[[朱印状]]を発給した。 [[慶長]]5年（[[1600年]]）、家康は神社近傍を流れる[[多摩川|六郷川]]に架橋（[[六郷橋|六郷大橋]]）を命じ[http://www.ktr.mlit.go.jp/kawakoku/pdf/oyabashira.pdf 旧六郷橋 親柱物語]、その竣工を祈って願文を奉り、またこの神社の[[神輿]]によって渡初式を行ったと伝えられている。このように徳川家との縁が深いため、神紋として八幡宮の[[巴紋]]と[[葵紋#三つ葉葵|三つ葉葵]]紋を用いている。 時期については定かではないが、六郷神社では元来、他の八幡社と同様に八幡三神（誉田別尊、神功皇后、比売大神）を祀っていた。 しかし、あるときの曳船祭で、一座の神輿が[[東京湾]]の対岸である[[上総国]]に流されてしまい、もう一座の神輿はたいへんな荒神で、しばしば祟りを受けたので土中に埋めてしまったと江戸時代の書に書かれている（[[享保]]4年（[[1719年]]）造営の本殿は三神を祀る建築様式になっていることから、上記の故事が起きたのはそれ以後ではないかとされている）。 江戸時代には、[[東海道]]を隔てた西側の[[宝珠院 (大田区)|宝珠院]]が[[別当寺]]であったが、[[明治維新]]により廃された。 [[明治]]5年（[[1872年]]）、東京府郷社となる。明治9年（[[1876年]]）に六郷神社と改称した。 [[昭和]]62年（[[1987年]]）、鎮座九百三十年祭執行。 ==境内== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|社殿]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|神橋]] * 社殿 : 昭和62年（1987年）の鎮座九百三十年を期に建て替えられたが、享保4年（1719年）の造営の本殿は改修された。 : 古くから本殿正面に掲げられた扁額の「八幡宮」の文字は、源忠持筆と記されている（年代不詳）。 * 神門 : 神橋を前にした切妻造。左右に透塀を連ねて玉垣をめぐらしている。 * 神橋 : 梶原景時の寄進と伝えられる石造の太鼓橋が残っている。 * 社務所 : [[平成]]7年（[[1995年]]）の造営。 *白旗の杉 : 拝殿のすぐ東側にあった白旗の杉は、樹齢1000年といわれ、御神木として崇められていたが、[[大正]]11年（[[1922年]]）に借しくも枯れ、現在は根株のみが残る。 *手水舎 : 切妻造。表参道の東側にある。 {{clear}} ==境内社== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|境内社]] * 稲荷神社 * 氷川神社 * 天祖神社 * 三柱神社 {{clear}} == 祭事 == [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|神楽殿]] === 例大祭 === ; 例祭 毎年6月3日に行われ、6月の第1金曜日の神社神輿遷御祭では、六郷14町会約40基の神輿が次々と神社に担ぎ込まれ、みたま入れが行われる。 翌土曜日には、数百年の伝統を持つ獅子舞の奉納が神楽殿で行われ、神獅子巡行、六郷ばやしの奉納がある。翌日曜日、御神幸祭で神輿に乗せたご神体が六郷14町会を巡行しクライマックスを迎える。 ; 宮神輿 古来より六郷神社の神輿ぶりは勇壮をもって知られており、一之神輿は、昭和7年（[[1932年]]）に新調したもので、台幅1.21m、高さ1.96m、重量450kg（担ぎ棒を含まず）。二之神輿は、[[文久]]3年（[[1863年]]）の古い神輿を、昭和55年（[[1980年]]）に氏子青年会が修復したものである。 === 流鏑馬 === ; 七草こども流鏑馬祭 毎年1月7日にこどもの開運・健康・出世を祈り、裃姿のこどもによって執り行われる。古くは弓射りとも称し、源頼朝の奉納に始まるという伝承がある。
社前に設けられた射場は、18本の青竹を矢来に組んでヨシズを張り、その上に八方白眼（はっぽうにらみ）という特有の的（それぞれ異なる8つの眉と目を縦2列に描いた和紙を、大きな蛇の目の中央に張ったもの）である。弓は椿の木に麻の弦を張ったもの、矢は長さ1m程の篠竹に西之内の矢羽を付けたものを用いる。 射士は氏子の12歳以下の男児に限られ、射る順番は抽選によって決められる。1番手と2番手が、矢を交差させ、3本の大きな御幣が立っている的の上を越すように射り（山越し）、山越しがすむと、3番手と4番手が『一の目玉』を、5番手と6番手が『二の目玉』という具合に射り進む。
流鏑馬といっても馬には乗らず、多摩川流域に多くみられた「歩射」（御備射）の伝統を守っていたが、平成10年（[[1998年]]）からは八方白眼の幕の前に置いた木馬にまたがり、正方形の杉板の的に鏑矢を射るという、新しい様式が取り入れられている。 === 過去に行われていた祭事 === ; 曳船祭 六郷神社の曳船祭は、江戸時代から六郷川（多摩川河口域）で勇壮華麗にくりひろげられ、関東三大船祭りの一つといわれた。かつて羽田と対岸の大師河原が六郷神社の氏子であったため、宮神輿を御座船に乗せ、四艘の足船が長さ150尺（約45m）の大綱で曳き、その後から各町会の迎え船と送り船がのぼり旗を風になびかせ、囃子の音と無数の観覧船とともに川を下っていった。 ==文化財== ;狛犬 : 大田区指定有形文化財（金石文）。昭和55年3月19日指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} :神楽殿の前庭に置かれている狛犬は、[[貞享]]2年（[[1685年]]）に六郷中町の有志が願主となり、二世安楽を祈って奉納したもので、大田区内最古の狛犬である。石工は三右衛門。元は社殿前にあった。左側の呵形は耳を付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約50cm。右側の吽形は角を3つ付け、高さ約45cm、胴長約52cm。 ;六郷神社獅子舞 :大田区指定無形民俗文化財。平成19年指定。{{sfn|大田区指定文化財一覧}} ; 六郷神社の流鏑馬 :東京都指定無形民俗文化財（民俗芸能）。昭和38年指定。{{sfn|六郷神社の流鏑馬}} === その他 === * 社宝四神 - 六郷八幡宮の四神については、[[天保]]3年（[[1831年]]）の『東都歳事記』（[[斎藤月岑]]著）にも記述がある。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} ; 注釈 ; 出典 {{reflist|1}} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.tokyo-jinjacho.or.jp/ota/200148/|title=六郷神社|publisher=東京都神社庁|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref=harv}} * {{Cite web|和書|title=大田区指定文化財一覧|website=大田区|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|大田区指定文化財一覧}} |url=https://www.city.ota.tokyo.jp/kyouiku/bunkazai/bunkazai_hogo/shitei_torokubunkazai/bunkazai_list.files/20190401bunka_ku_shitei.pdf}} * {{Cite web|和書|url=https://bunkazai.metro.tokyo.lg.jp/jp/search_detail.html?page=1&id=478|title=六郷神社の流鏑馬|website=東京都文化財情報データベース|accessdate=2020-7-27|ref={{sfnref|六郷神社の流鏑馬}}}} == 関連項目 == {{Commonscat}} ==外部リンク== *[http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html 六郷神社公式サイト] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:ろくこうしんしや}} [[Category:大田区の神社]] [[Category:武蔵国の式内社]] [[Category:東京都の旧郷社]] [[Category:八幡宮]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name=Rokugō Shrine | Image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | Location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===Annual Festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

{{Shrine | Name=Rokugō Shrine | Image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | Location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===Annual Festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | Location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===Annual Festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]][[ja:六郷神社]]

Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name=Rokugō Shrine | Image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | Location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===Annual Festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | Deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}}. Its former rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary. ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * **Main Hall:** Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * **Shinmon:** A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * **Sacred Bridge:** A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * **Shrine Office:** Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} 7 (1995). * **White Flag Cedar:** A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * **Chōzuya (Purification Basin):** Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * **Guardian Dogs (Komainu):** Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * **Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * **Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:** Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Rokugō Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:六郷神社]]

In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
