It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|12=draft|12=draft}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|12=draft|12=draft}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|12=draft|12=draft}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=draft|12=draft}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=draft|12=draft}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|12=draft|12=draft}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=draft|12=draft}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|12=draft|12=draft}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|12=draft|12=draft}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|12=draft|12=draft}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|12=draft|12=draft}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|12=draft|12=draft}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|12=draft|12=draft}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|12=draft|12=draft}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|12=draft|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|12=draft|12=draft}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|12=draft|12=draft}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|12=draft|12=draft}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|12=draft|12=draft}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|12=draft|12=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=draft|12=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=draft|12=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|12=draft|12=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=draft|12=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=draft|12=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|12=draft|12=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|12=draft|12=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|12=draft|12=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|12=draft|12=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|12=draft|12=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=draft|12=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

#redirect[[:en:六郷神社]] [[Category:automatic wikipedia redirects 六]]

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}}

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gōsha Shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha shrines in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Former Gō-sha shrines" to "Former Gō-sha"
revision.text:
{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyocho|qq=draft|qq=draft}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|qq=}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|qq=}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|qq=}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshiie|qq=}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|qq=}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|qq=}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Nine Years' War|qq=}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunji|qq=}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qq=}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Fujiwara|qq=}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshū War|qq=}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkyū|qq=}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajiwara Kagetoki|qq=}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|qq=}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|qq=}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō|qq=}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|qq=}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|qq=}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō|qq=}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō|qq=}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|qq=}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|qq=}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|qq=}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|qq=}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshiie|qq=}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|qq=}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|qq=}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Nine Years' War|qq=}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunji|qq=}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qq=}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Fujiwara|qq=}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshū War|qq=}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkyū|qq=}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajiwara Kagetoki|qq=}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|qq=}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|qq=}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō|qq=}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|qq=}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|qq=}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō|qq=}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō|qq=}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|qq=}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Rokugō Shrine]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenki|qq=}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Ward|qq=}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|qq=}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|qq=}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|qq=}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshiie|qq=}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|qq=}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|qq=}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Nine Years' War|qq=}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunji|qq=}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qq=}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Fujiwara|qq=}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshū War|qq=}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkyū|qq=}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajiwara Kagetoki|qq=}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|qq=}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|qq=}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō|qq=}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|qq=}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|qq=}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō|qq=}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō|qq=}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|qq=}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]

{{Shrine | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=[[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png|260px]]
Torii, Shinmon, and Haiden
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=13|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=260|coord={{coord|35|32|39|N|139|42|47|E}}}} | location=[[Tokyo]], [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Emperor Ōjin|ja|誉田別尊|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Emperor Ōjin|qq=}}{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|qq=}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tenki|ja|天喜|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenki|qq=}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city }} '''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|ja|神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrine|qq=}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|qq=}}, {{ill|Ōta Ward|ja|大田区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōta Ward|qq=}}, Tokyo. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian period|qq=}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern shrine Ranking system|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern shrine Ranking system|13=User:Immanuelle/Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|qq=}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|ja|武蔵国|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Province|qq=}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|qq=}}.The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical. The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|qq=}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman shrine|lt=Hachiman Shrine|ja|八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Shrine|qq=}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|ja|神功皇后|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Empress Jingū|qq=}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|qq=}}, are not included. ==History== ===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|ja|源義家|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshiie|qq=}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|ja|源頼義|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|qq=}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachimangū|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|ja|石清水八幡宮|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|qq=}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Early Nine Years' War|ja|前九年の役|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Early Nine Years' War|qq=}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū." ===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji|ja|文治|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bunji|qq=}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|ja|源頼朝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoritomo|qq=}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|ja|源義経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Yoshitsune|qq=}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|ja|奥州藤原氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Fujiwara|qq=}} during the {{ill|Ōshū War|ja|奥州合戦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōshū War|qq=}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|ja|建久|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenkyū|qq=}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|ja|梶原景時|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kajiwara Kagetoki|qq=}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate. In {{ill|Tenshō|ja|天正|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)|lt=Tenshō Era|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenshō (Momoyama period)|qq=}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|ja|徳川家康|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokugawa Ieyasu|qq=}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|1=Keichō|2=ja|3=慶長|ja|慶長|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Keichō|qq=}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|qq=}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|qq=}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi. Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|ja|享保|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyōhō|qq=}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined. During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|qq=}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|qq=}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō|ja|東海道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōkaidō|qq=}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji Restoration|qq=}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876). In {{ill|Shōwa|ja|昭和|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōwa|qq=}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heisei|qq=}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō|ja|大正|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taishō|qq=}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach. ==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine ==Festivals== [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]] ===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|qq=}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday. ===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual. ===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto. ==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963. ==Notes== {{notelist}} ==References== {{Reflist}} ==See Also== * {{Commonscat}} ==External Links== * [http://www.rokugo.or.jp/index2.html Official Site of Rokugō Shrine] {{translated page|ja|六郷神社|version=103151548|comment=Imported full ja history}} {{draft categories| [[Category: ==Precincts== [[File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shaden.png|thumb|240px|right|Main Shrine Building]] [[Category:Shrines in Ōta Ward]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Musashi Province]] [[Category:Former Gō-sha in Tokyo]] [[Category:Hachiman Shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fix draft categories]] }} [[sn:Rokugō Shrine]] [[ja:六郷神社]] [[en:Rokugō Shrine]]
