'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Hōju-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.

===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215324]] Wikidata name: Rokugo-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Hōju-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.

===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.

Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Rokugo-jinja]][[ja:六郷神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q17215324]] Wikidata name: Rokugo-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Hōju-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.

[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Hōju-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Hōju-in|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki|1=Tengi}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234|1=Ōta, Tokyo}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward|1=Ōta, Tokyo}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji|1=Bunji (era)}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara|1=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War|1=Battle of Ōshū}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū|1=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki|1=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Q11452623|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (region)}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name=Rokugō Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image=File:Rokugo-Jinja, Shinmon.png | location={{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, [[Ōta Ward]], [[Higashi-Rokugō]] 3-10-18{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | ISO=JP-13 | Latitude_deg=35| Latitude_min=32| Latitude_sec=39.1 | Longitude_deg=139| Longitude_min=42| Longitude_sec=47.2 | deity={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}} | Rank=Former {{ill|Gōsha|ja|郷社|1=Gō-sha|WD=Q134917316}}{{efn|District Shrines|WD=Q90139226|lt=Ken-sha|1=modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gōsha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近代社格制度|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q712534}} | Foundation=Traditionally {{ill|Tengi|en|Tengi|WD=Q1193591|lt=Tenki|1=Tengi}} 5 (1057){{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Main_Hall= | Alias= | Pilgrimage= | Annual_Festival=[[June 3]]{{sfn|Tokyo Shrine Bureau}} | Rituals=[[Yabusame]] Festival (January 7) | Map=Japan Tokyo city | prefecture={{ill|Ota|WD=Q217234|1=Ōta, Tokyo}} | coordinates={{Coord|35.54416694444444|N|139.71305611111111|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Rokugō Shrine''' (六郷神社) is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in {{ill|Higashi-Rokugō|ja|東六郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Rokugō|1=Higashi-Rokugō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11526065}}, {{ill|Ōta, Tokyo|en|Ōta, Tokyo|WD=Q217234|lt=Ōta Ward|1=Ōta, Tokyo}}, Tokyo. It is regarded as the guardian shrine for the entire Rokugō area. The shrine's origins are ancient, dating back to the late {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. Its former Rank under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|en|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|WD=Q712534|lt=Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}} was Gōsha. The shrine is mentioned in the {{ill|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|ja|延喜式神名帳|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|2=ja|3=延喜式神名帳|4=zh|5=延喜式神名帳|6=simple|7=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|13=User:Immanuelle/Engishiki Jinmyōchō|qq=}} as "Hiyata Shrine" (薭田神社), located in {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi Province|1=Musashi Province}}, {{ill|Ebara District|ja|荏原郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ebara District|1=Ebara district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1278470}}.<ref group="note">The first character of "Hiyata" is "稗" with a grass radical.</ref>
The enshrined deity is {{ill|Hachiman Ōkami|ja|八幡大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachiman Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 八幡神|1=Hachiman|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q261637}} (Emperor Ōjin). While it is a {{ill|Hachiman shrine|en|Hachiman shrine|WD=Q11390939|lt=Hachiman Shrine|1=Hachiman shrine}}, only Emperor Ōjin is enshrined today; the other two deities of Hachiman, {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|WD=Q232803|lt=Empress Jingū|1=Empress Jingū}} and {{ill|Hime no Ōkami|ja|比売大神|lt=Hime-no-Ōkami|1=Himegami|3=比売神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hime no Ōkami|13=User:Immanuelle/Himegami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 比売神}}, are not included.
==History==
===Foundation=== According to shrine tradition, in Tenki 5 (1057), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshiie|en|Minamoto no Yoshiie|WD=Q32613|lt=Minamoto no Yoshiie|1=Minamoto no Yoshiie}} and his father {{ill|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|en|Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|WD=Q3082851|lt=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi|1=Minamoto no Yoriyoshi}} raised the Genji white flag atop a great cedar in this area to rally their troops. They prayed for military success at {{ill|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|en|Iwashimizu Hachimangū|WD=Q710098|lt=Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū|1=Iwashimizu Hachimangū}}, resulting in high morale and victory in the {{ill|Former Nine Years' War|en|Former Nine Years' War|WD=Q1187448|lt=Early Nine Years' War|1=Former Nine Years' War}}. Upon their triumphant return, they enshrined a divided spirit of Iwashimizu Hachiman-gū here, establishing the Hachiman Shrine. During the Edo period, the shrine was called "Rokugō Hachiman-gū."
===Historical Development=== In {{ill|Bunji (era)|en|Bunji (era)|WD=Q1194415|lt=Bunji|1=Bunji (era)}} 5 (1189), {{ill|Minamoto no Yoritomo|en|Minamoto no Yoritomo|WD=Q242800|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|1=Minamoto no Yoritomo}} also prayed here for success in his campaign against {{ill|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|en|Minamoto no Yoshitsune|WD=Q310445|lt=Minamoto no Yoshitsune|1=Minamoto no Yoshitsune}} and the {{ill|Northern Fujiwara|en|Northern Fujiwara|WD=Q1050942|lt=Northern Fujiwara|1=Northern Fujiwara}} during the {{ill|Battle of Ōshū|en|Battle of Ōshū|WD=Q11445749|lt=Ōshū War|1=Battle of Ōshū}}. In {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|WD=Q1193933|lt=Kenkyū|1=Kenkyū}} 2 (1191), he ordered {{ill|Kajiwara Kagetoki|en|Kajiwara Kagetoki|WD=Q3191855|lt=Kajiwara Kagetoki|1=Kajiwara Kagetoki}} to rebuild the shrine buildings. The lion's head now kept as a treasure and the purification stone in the precinct were offerings from Yoritomo, while Kagetoki is said to have donated the drum bridge in front of the shrine gate.
In {{ill|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|en|Tenshō (Momoyama period)|WD=Q1145058|lt=Tenshō Era|1=Tenshō (Momoyama period)}} 19 (1591), {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} issued a red-seal land grant of 18 koku to the shrine. In {{ill|Keichō|en|Keichō|WD=Q1328185|lt=Keichō|1=Keichō}} 5 (1600), he ordered the construction of a bridge over the nearby {{ill|Rokugō River|ja|六郷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō River|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 多摩川|1=Tama River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1063006}} (Tama River) and prayed for its completion at the shrine. The inauguration of the bridge, called the {{ill|Rokugō Bridge|ja|六郷橋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Bridge|1=Rokugo Bridge|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11392598}}, featured the shrine’s mikoshi.
Over time, like other Hachiman Shrines, Rokugō Shrine originally enshrined Hachiman's three deities. However, Edo-period records describe an incident during the Hikifune Festival where one mikoshi drifted to the other side of Tokyo Bay (Kazusa Province), and another was deemed too dangerous and buried. By the time of the {{ill|Kyōhō|en|Kyōhō|WD=Q1145048|lt=Kyōhō|1=Kyōhō}} 4 (1719) construction of the main hall, only Emperor Ōjin was enshrined.
During the Edo period, the shrine’s {{ill|Bettō-ji|ja|別当寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Bettō-ji|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} was {{ill|Hōju-in|ja|宝珠院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in|1=Q11452623|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452623}}, located on the west side of the {{ill|Tōkaidō (region)|en|Tōkaidō (region)|WD=Q1196306|lt=Tōkaidō|1=Tōkaidō (region)}} road. Hōju-in was abolished during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}. In Meiji 5 (1872), the shrine was designated as a Tokyo Prefecture Gōsha and renamed Rokugō Shrine in Meiji 9 (1876).
In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. It died in {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|WD=Q329174|lt=Taishō|1=Taishō era}} 11 (1922), and only its rootstock remains. * '''Chōzuya (Purification Basin):''' Located on the east side of the approach.
==Sub-Shrines== [[File:RokugoJinja,Keidaisha.png|thumb|240px|right|Sub-Shrines]] * Inari Shrine * Hikawa Shrine * Tenso Shrine * Mihashira Shrine
==Festivals==
[[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
===festival=== The shrine’s annual festival is held on June 3, with the mikoshi procession starting on the first Friday of June. Forty mikoshi from the 14 town councils of Rokugō gather at the shrine for purification. Traditional lion dances, the {{ill|Rokugō Hayashi|ja|六郷ばやし|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rokugō Hayashi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and the shrine's mikoshi parade culminate in the main event on Sunday.
===Yabusame Festival=== The "Nanakusa Kodomo Yabusame Festival" is held annually on January 7 to pray for children’s health, success, and well-being. Boys under 12 participate in this traditional ritual.
===Discontinued Festivals=== The Hikifune Festival, once a grand spectacle on the Rokugō River, was one of the three great boat festivals of Kanto.
==Cultural Properties== * '''Guardian Dogs (Komainu):''' Designated tangible cultural property by Ōta Ward. * '''Rokugō Shrine Lion Dance:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Ōta Ward in 2007. * '''Yabusame of Rokugō Shrine:''' Designated intangible cultural heritage by Tokyo in 1963.

In {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|WD=Q327567|lt=Shōwa|1=Shōwa era}} 62 (1987), the shrine celebrated its 930th anniversary.[[File:RokugoJinja Shinbashi.png|thumb|240px|right|Sacred Bridge]] * '''Main Hall:''' Rebuilt during the 930th-anniversary celebrations in 1987, though the Kyōhō-era (1719) structure was renovated and preserved. * '''Shinmon:''' A gabled structure in front of the sacred bridge, with tamagaki fencing extending to the sides. * '''Sacred Bridge:''' A stone drum bridge believed to have been donated by Kajiwara Kagetoki. * '''Shrine Office:''' Rebuilt in {{ill|Heisei era|en|Heisei era|WD=Q234510|lt=Heisei|1=Heisei era}} 7 (1995). * '''White Flag Cedar:''' A sacred tree, said to be over 1,000 years old, located east of the main hall. [[File:Kagura-den of Rokugo-jinja.png|thumb|240px|right|Kagura Hall]]
